hero.label: Product Intelligence & Execution
hero.body: A few structured conversations with the Trio and the system does the rest — research, validation, prototype, and a product direction you can trust.

heading: Most teams build
what's requested.

items.label: The Requirement Problem
items.body: Clients know their domain. They do not know what product will serve that domain best. The requirement is always a hypothesis — often a good one, rarely the final answer.

items.label: The Translation Problem
items.body: Between the client's intent and the development team lies a gap. Meetings, tickets, misunderstandings, rework. Most of the waste in software projects lives in this gap.

items.label: The Timing Problem
items.body: By the time most teams discover the product direction was wrong, months have passed. The prototype comes after the contract, not before it.

problem.pullQuoteBody: The intake session is not requirement gathering. It is domain research. By the time it concludes, the Trio knows your space — well enough to prescribe.
problem.footnote: Grounded in Evans' Domain-Driven Design, Christensen's Jobs-to-Be-Done, and the British Design Council's Double Diamond framework.

stages.title: Intelligent Outreach
stages.body: Before you reach us, the system is already working. Ad framings, positioning signals, and market context — continuously refined.

stages.title: Structured Intake
stages.body: You book. You provide context. The system immediately begins domain research and produces a briefing document for the Trio — before the first session.

stages.title: Deep Sessions
stages.body: A small number of high-value conversations. The Trio clarifies, challenges, and expands. The goal is clarity — not prolonged discussion. The system supports in real time.

stages.title: Parallel Build
stages.body: While sessions happen, prototypes are materializing. The domain model is made concrete. By alignment, a working demo exists.

stages.title: Convergence
stages.body: Requirements finalize. Demo is ready. Product direction is clear. Next steps are obvious. Not because we were lucky — because the process was designed this way.

cards.title: Internal Use
cards.body: The system knows the Trio's backgrounds, past work, and market signals. It generates positioning strategies and advertisement framings that the Trio can brainstorm against — sharpening the message through dialogue with the system itself.
cards.footer: This is how you found us.

cards.title: Client Use
cards.body: Once a product is being built, the same capability is instantiated fresh for the client's domain. It does not reuse the Trio's logic — it rebuilds from the client's market context, target users, and the job the product does. The result: the product is not just built. It is introduced correctly.
cards.footer: Your product, positioned from day one.

trio.label: The People
heading: Three roles.
One unit.
trio.closingQuote: Three complementary capabilities. One position. One product.

members.title: System Architect
members.body:
Thinks in systems before he thinks in code. The Lead's role is conceptual integrity — ensuring that what gets built reflects reality accurately, not just what the client requested.

He is the primary knowledge cruncher in every intake session. The one who, by the session's end, can say with confidence: this is what you need, and this is why.

page.heroSubtitle: Thinks in systems before he thinks in code. The primary knowledge cruncher in every intake session.

philosophy.label: Philosophy
heading: Conceptual integrity
above all else.
philosophy.quote: The requirement is always a hypothesis — often a good one, rarely the final answer.
philosophy.quoteBody: The Lead doesn't take requirements at face value. Every intake session is domain research — a structured excavation of what actually needs to exist.

items.title: Domain First
items.body: Before any architecture decision, understand the domain deeply. Software that reflects the domain accurately is software that works.

items.title: Conceptual Integrity
items.body: A system designed by one mind — or a small, aligned group — will be coherent. Coherent systems are maintainable, explainable, and trusted.

items.title: Prescribe, Don't Transcribe
items.body: The client knows their domain. They don't know the solution space. The Lead's role is to prescribe what should be built — and articulate why.

items.title: Knowledge Crunching
items.body: Every conversation is an opportunity to refine the model. The domain model isn't a diagram — it's a living understanding that improves with every session.

stages.label: Before the Session
stages.body: The system prepares a domain briefing. The Lead reviews it, identifies gaps, and formulates the questions that will drive the conversation.

stages.label: During the Session
stages.body: The Lead is the primary listener and questioner. Every answer is processed through the lens of domain modeling — what entities exist, what relationships matter, what the real constraints are.

stages.label: After the Session
stages.body: The domain model is refined. The Lead hands a clear architectural direction to the Apprentice, who ensures it survives implementation. The Brute builds it.

closing.quote: This is what you need, and this is why.
closing.attribution: — The Lead, at the end of every intake session

members.title: Integrator
members.body:
Lives at the intersection of classical and agentic development. He has written production code in the traditional sense and orchestrated AI agents in the new sense — and crucially, understands the difference between them.

The Apprentice ensures that the Lead's domain model survives contact with implementation, and that the Brute's output meets the standard the model requires.

page.heroSubtitle: The bridge between conceptual design and working code. Classical engineering meets agentic execution.

philosophy.label: Philosophy
heading: The model must survive
contact with reality.
philosophy.quote: A beautiful architecture that can't be implemented is just a drawing. An implementation that ignores the architecture is just a hack.
philosophy.quoteBody: The Apprentice holds both truths simultaneously — ensuring the domain model lands in code without compromise, and that the code earns the trust of the model.

items.title: Translate, Don't Dilute
items.body: The domain model from the Lead is precise for a reason. The Apprentice's job is to translate it into implementation without losing fidelity.

items.title: Classical When It Matters
items.body: Not everything should be delegated to agents. The Apprentice knows when hand-written code is the right choice — and when agentic tooling multiplies output.

items.title: Quality Gate
items.body: Every output from the Brute passes through the Apprentice's judgment. Speed without standards is just fast failure.

items.title: Integration Is the Product
items.body: Components that work in isolation but fail together are worthless. The Apprentice owns the seams — the places where subsystems meet.

stages.label: During the Session
stages.body: The Apprentice listens for implementation risk. While the Lead models the domain, the Apprentice is already mapping it to technical constraints — what's feasible, what's fragile, what needs a different approach.

stages.label: During the Build
stages.body: Translates the Lead's architecture into specifications the Brute can execute. Reviews output continuously. Catches drift between intent and implementation before it compounds.

stages.label: At Convergence
stages.body: Owns the integration. Ensures that separately built components compose correctly, that the domain model is faithfully represented, and that the product works as a whole.

closing.quote: The architecture is only as good as its implementation. The implementation is only as good as its architecture.
closing.attribution: — The Apprentice, on why both sides matter

members.title: Executor
members.body:
No vocabulary of classical programming. Total fluency in AI-native execution. Given a specification, the Brute owns the process of producing it — rapidly, reliably, using the full capability of agentic development tools.

Primarily: UI, interaction, and frontend. With specification from the Lead or Apprentice: backend and infrastructure. This scope expands as the tools do.

page.heroSubtitle: Total fluency in AI-native execution. Given a specification, the Brute owns the process of producing it — rapidly and reliably.

philosophy.label: Philosophy
heading: Specification in,
product out.
philosophy.quote: Speed is not the goal. Speed is the byproduct of removing everything that isn't building.
philosophy.quoteBody: The Brute doesn't plan, doesn't debate, doesn't second-guess. The specification has already been refined by the Lead and the Apprentice. What remains is execution — and execution is what the Brute does.

items.title: Trust the Specification
items.body: The spec comes from the Lead through the Apprentice. It has been challenged, refined, and validated. The Brute's job is to realize it — not to question it.

items.title: Agentic-Native
items.body: Every tool, every workflow, every shortcut that AI-native development offers — the Brute uses it. Not as an experiment, but as the primary mode of production.

items.title: Output Over Process
items.body: No standups, no status updates, no ceremony. The demo speaks. If it works, the process was right. If it doesn't, the next iteration is already underway.

items.title: Scope Expands With Tooling
items.body: Today: UI, interaction, frontend. Tomorrow: whatever the agentic tools can reliably produce. The Brute's capability ceiling is the tooling's capability ceiling.

stages.label: Receiving the Spec
stages.body: The Apprentice delivers a clear, validated specification. The Brute reviews it for completeness — not for correctness. Correctness is the upstream team's responsibility.

stages.label: During the Build
stages.body: Rapid, parallel execution using agentic development tools. UI materializes. Interactions take shape. Prototypes emerge while the intake sessions are still happening.

stages.label: At Demo
stages.body: The working product is the deliverable. Not a mockup, not a wireframe — a functional demo that the client can touch, test, and respond to.

closing.quote: The demo is ready. It was ready before you asked.

frameworks.label: The Foundation
heading: Every claim we make
is grounded.
frameworks.subheading: We did not invent these ideas. We are the first team operating all of them simultaneously, at agentic speed.

tiles.label: Domain-Driven Design · Evans, 2003
tiles.body: Developers who become domain experts are the most effective builders. The intake session is knowledge crunching — and it ends when the Trio knows the domain.
tiles.source: Eric Evans' official DDD resource hub — the primary home for the methodology.

tiles.label: Jobs-to-Be-Done · Christensen, Harvard
tiles.claim: The real need behind the stated requirement
tiles.body: Clients are experts in their own experience. They are not experts in the solution space. The Trio excavates the job beneath the requirement — and builds for that.
tiles.source: Original peer-reviewed article published by Harvard Business Review, September 2016.

tiles.label: Double Diamond · British Design Council, 2005
tiles.claim: Problem definition is half the work
tiles.body: Teams that define the right problem before building produce better outcomes. The Trio's intake is the first diamond — run before most teams start theirs.
tiles.source: The Design Council's own page for their own framework. Nothing more authoritative exists.

tiles.label: Lean Startup · Ries, 2011
tiles.claim: The prototype is a hypothesis, not a promise
tiles.body: The parallel build during intake is not a bet on correct requirements. It is the Build-Measure-Learn loop run at the intake stage — making the alignment conversation concrete.
tiles.source: Eric Ries' official Lean Startup methodology page — the principles, in full, for free.

tiles.label: The Trusted Advisor · Maister, Green & Galford
tiles.claim: Trust is earned through demonstrated competence
tiles.body: Trustworthiness = (Credibility + Reliability + Intimacy) ÷ Self-Orientation. The intake system is designed to address all four variables directly.
tiles.source: The Trust Equation — explained in full on the authors' own firm's website.

tiles.label: Agentic Coding Trends · Anthropic, 2026
tiles.claim: Small teams with agentic tools outperform large teams
tiles.body: One engineer can now maintain systems that previously required teams. The Trio's architecture — Lead, Apprentice, Brute — is designed around this reality.
tiles.source: Anthropic's official 2026 Agentic Coding Trends Report. Note: download requires an email.

portfolio.label: Work
heading: Built by the Trio.
portfolio.subheading: Products we've shipped — from intake to deployment.

projects.title: Trio Landing — This Website
projects.description: The product intelligence and execution landing page. Domain-driven content, AI-assisted build pipeline, and a booking system connected to a Cloudflare Worker backend.

cta.label: Ready
heading: A few conversations.
A working product.
cta.body: Book a session with the Trio. You'll provide context. We'll prepare. By the time we meet, the system has already begun working on your problem.
cta.footnote: No retainer. No discovery fee. One focused engagement.
